answer:
**Vṛhaspatitattva** opens with a foundational metaphysical distinction between *cetana* (conscious principle) and *acetana* (unconscious principle) as the twofold supreme *tattva* (reality) from which all other principles derive: "*Cetana* is that which has the nature of knowledge, which knows and is never subject to forgetting, which is eternally aware at all times, and which is unobscured. That is called *cetana*. *Acetana* is that which is without knowledge, like a stone. That is called *acetana*. When *cetana* and *acetana* meet, they give rise to all the *tattvas*."[^1] This *cetana* (*ātmā* or soul) is pure consciousness but becomes bound in *saṃsāra* (cyclic existence) through staining by *acetana* elements like *māyā* (illusion) and *pradhāna* (primordial matter), causing forgetfulness (*lupa*) and descent into lower states (human, animal, plant).[^2]

Charles Comte, in his treatment of liberty as essential to rights and duties inherent in human nature, presumes man as a self-aware "person" whose faculties develop naturally toward well-being unless obstructed: "liberty... simply designates the state of a person who encounters, in his fellow men, no obstacle, whether to the regular development of his being, or to the innocent exercise of his faculties."[^3] Dunoyer similarly posits man as an agent endowed *a priori* with "intelligence, a will, and organs," whose liberty is the acquired "power... to use his forces more easily as he frees himself from the obstacles that originally hindered their exercise," presupposing rational self-direction amid needs.[^4]

**Vṛhaspatitattva** would refuse Comte's category of the human "person" as an unproblematically self-aware, rational subject whose liberty flows inherently from nature toward moral and productive ends. For Vṛhaspati, the *ātmā* in human form is *not* presumed eternally self-aware or rational; it is "dazed consciousness" (*ātmā*), bent downward in *māyā*, forgetful due to pervasive *acetana* (*pradhāna* causes sleep-like oblivion), and prone to *tāmasa* (dark, inert) states yielding animal rebirths or worse.[^5] Comte's rational agent, developing via property and liberty, ignores this *acetana*-induced bondage; true *cetana* requires yogic discernment to dispel unconscious veils, not mere removal of social obstacles.[^6]

[^1]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, [Chapter 1, paragraph p-04d69c](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-04d69c) (and immediate context, [p-c31df3](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-c31df3)).

[^2]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, [Chapter 1, paragraphs p-643885 to p-695576](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-643885).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 3, paragraph p-1c5676](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-1c5676).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Chapter 1, paragraphs p-9ef4d3 and p-fc8999](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-9ef4d3).

[^5]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, [Chapter 1, paragraphs p-643885 and p-72edcc](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-643885).

[^6]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, [Chapter 1, paragraphs p-72a66e to p-7f98f9](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-72a66e) (on *ṣaḍaṅgayoga* for realizing *cetana*).

cetana OR acetana OR conscious OR unconscious OR "self-aware" OR "self-conscious"
